sentences.text: --give you that.
These are May '39.
[GERMAN] What--
On the 13th of May.
Doesn't say what the ship was doing.
We used to enter in at the Southampton--
Yeah, and nothing to go through, because he just--
It was-- we, yeah, right.
No.
Philip Freund, F-R-E-U-N-D.
Philip.
Oh, P-H-I-L-I-P.
Middle initial is S, as in Sigfried.
I was born the 5th of June, 1931, in the city of Munich, Bavaria, Germany.
Well, probably one of my earliest memories was that all of a sudden, we couldn't go to the park.
I couldn't ride the streetcar.
We couldn't go to the movies.
We were on half rations.
And food-- so food was scarce.
And there was a tremendous amount of anxiety and stress in the home.
And then-- I started going to public school.
And I was kicked out of there because I was Jewish.
I went to a-- to a Jewish school.
And that was burned down on Crystal Night in November of 1938.
Mhm.
I was five, maybe six, yeah.
I have a sister, a younger sister.
She's three years younger, Lisa.
Her name now is Avedon, yeah.
We were-- well, a family of three.
And then, we-- then, when Lisa came, we were a family of four-- my father, my mother, sister, and myself.
And we lived in Munich on Elisabethstrasse 19, in a large building.
We-- I had-- I shared room with my sister, if I recall.
And we had a, a good-sized unit there.
We, we rented.
Non-Jewish.
No, it was an integrated-- well, my grandmother lived a block down and-- on Tengstrasse.
And then across the street was my grandmother's brother.
He lived across the street.
And down the same street, her other brother, [?
Willy, ?] lived across the street.
And Hugo lived down the street, oh, about maybe 8, 10 blocks.
So we were-- there was-- it was a large family of my-- on my mother's side.
There was no one on my father's side left except his brother, who lived in Frankfurt.
And why was there no one left?
Well, he just had a brother.
His, his mother and father died in the 1920s.
And I was born in 1931.
So I didn't even know those people.
Close-knit.
My mother was a very caring person, although [LAUGHS] she loved to socialize.
And she, you know-- typical German woman who-- she went to her afternoon coffees down at-- in Prinzregentenstrasse.
And she would meet with her mother or her friends.
And I had a-- we had a governess who took care of us.
And we had a maid.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that's how we lived.
[LAUGHS]
Yes, a prosperous Jewish family.
We-- in the summers, we went to Tegernsee, which is a lake south of Munich.
They rented a home there.
And we were on a lake.
And I, I can remember going swimming in the lake and walking up in the mountains and, and doing things.
In fact, I can recall a tremendously rainy summer and watching the, the main stream going through the little town where we stayed and watching trees come floating down.
So it-- yeah, we had a-- we had a very nice life.
My family owned-- my grandfather started a company called Becker and Rokel in Munich in the year 1900, and then took in his two brothers-in-law.
And they were-- did printing, printing cards and also placards for advertisements.
And there was quite a bit of money there.
We were certainly not lacking for anything.
Rokel, R-O with an umlaut K-E-L.
My-- I'm sorry.
My father was the sales manager for the company.
He, he worked there.
And then-- it's interesting.
In 1951, I went back to, to Germany and went-- called them up in Munich and told them who I was.
And they said, we'll send the car for you.
And I went back to the factory.
And when I got to the-- to the plant, the people who knew me as a little boy lined up in the driveway.
And I had to walk, walk down the line, shaking everyone's hands and walked into the office.
And my father's secretary still worked there.
And it just broke her up.
I spent the whole day there, walking around the plant, eating with the workers.
And they were all very sorry that we-- the family no longer owned the plant.
OK.
Right.
We would-- we would go away.
We would go, and we would do things in Munich, as far as I can recall, going downtown.
And we would go to Englischer Garten, which is a very large park in the city of Munich.
And on Sunday afternoons, we'd listen to the music.
And then at the other end of Elisabethstrasse was Warner Kaserne, which is-- it's now a US military base.
But I, I watched the German soldiers on maneuvers out there, planes landing.
Now that whole area is part of the Olympic village in Munich.
Not really.
We had a Christmas tree.
[LAUGHS] With-- I remember we had candles.
My mother always said it was primarily for the-- for the maid and the governess.
But I remember getting, getting presents at that time, during Christmas.
I had a train-- electric train that, that I played with.
We were-- they did belong to synagogue.
And the, the rabbi from Munich also emigrated and ended up in New York City and started a congregation in New York City.
And my mother and grandmother joined.
That same congregation that was originally in Munich is now in New York City.
And the Rabbi Baerwald, he did my bar mitzvah.
Yes, absolutely.
Absolutely.
My father was an officer in the Kaiser's Army in World War I.
And he was accepted.
And we got along very well with, with-- as far as I could tell, with the people-- with everyone else in Germany.
There was no problems.
As soon as Hitler came to power, and they started restricting the movements and what privileges and rights we had.
In 1935, they took away our citizenship.
So we were fair game.
We had no more legal recourse because we were no longer citizens of Germany.
I don't remember any conversations, no.
But I, I do remember that all of a sudden, we couldn't get on the streetcar, And we couldn't go to the park.
And we couldn't go to the movies.
And I remember wanting to, to see some of the movies, but my mother said we couldn't go.
I believe so.
I just don't remember.
Well, my, my father was still-- at that time-- in, in-- up until 1937, was still working in the-- in the company.
And he would travel all over Europe.
And he would-- then would bring back food from Sweden.
I remember him bringing cheese and butter and eggs from Sweden so that that supplemented our, our food.
But if he wouldn't have traveled, I-- we would have really been very hungry.
So we really didn't have much food.
Anna, the governess, took me to the Catholic church, which was around the corner.
And the priest gave-- I remember him giving me-- after the Christmas service, giving me an orange.
And I had never seen an orange before.
And I had no idea even how to eat the thing.
And then Anna showed me that you have to peel it and, and eat it.
So we were really lacking all of a sudden, from living fairly well to living very poorly because of the lack of food.
And there were travel restrictions.
And then the money was also-- my mother's bank account was [GERMAN].

That means blocked-- so that she couldn't have access to her money without going and getting permission from the Gestapo The fact that you had-- that you had rations-- that you were rationed because you were-- you were Jews?
Well, everyone was rationed, except we had-- we were on half rations.
They would give us a half a ration book rather than the whole ration book.
I remember that because I, I would go shopping with the maid.
And she, she said, well, we can only get so much.
And I said, well, why are these other people getting twice as much, you know, much more than we are?
And she said, well, this is all we have coupons for.
And then I-- when I got home, my mother told me that we only were also authorized half rations.
And there was a big red J marked on our ID cards as well as on the ration book.
Discriminated against, that's right, simply because we were Jews.
No.
I know that he would-- he would come and go.

I know that after-- a long time afterwards, my mother told me that he knew Count Bernadotte and-- who wanted him to, to come to Sweden and start up a printing plant there and that he would put up the money.
And my father would put up expertise.
And my father told him that he couldn't do that because he was German.
And he had served in the Kaiser's Army in World War I.
And he was not about to, to leave Germany.
In Sweden.
Married to royalty, yeah.
He, he was part of the royalty.
I don't-- he was probably married to royalty.
But he also came out of the royal house himself.
And then he was-- he-- he'd saved from Jews in World War-- you know, during-- before and during World War II.
And then he-- also, he then died in, in an explosion in, in the King David Hotel in Jerusalem in 1948, I believe.
Yes.
I must have been six, six, six years old.
Yes.
I started school there.
And then things, things didn't-- all of a sudden got very bad.
Kids started beating up on us.
There were other Jewish kids also in the school.
And the teacher was not very kind.
I remember that.
And there was a lot of stress.
Then my father died.
And that didn't help the situation.
And then I stayed in that school until November of 1938.

But I had a difficult time with the lessons because of the stress and, I guess, lack of food and, and because the other kids really were very unkind to, to all the Jewish kids-- not just to myself, but to all the other ones.
Mm-hmm, physically beaten, spit upon, called 'dirty Jew,' ink thrown on my-- I was learning how to write and read.
Ink thrown on, on, on my books-- it was just nasty kids, you know.
On the playground, they'd, they'd kick you.
They'd, they'd throw things at you.
On the way to school, they pushed you into the gutter, that type of thing.
Nothing.
He'd laugh.
He thought it was funny.
If I remember that-- I remember him standing there.
I mean, he was quite a tall man.
And I complained that somebody had poured ink on my-- on my writing paper.
Because I was copying the letters from the blackboard in this German script, and I had a difficult time.
The kid next to me was constantly bothering me.
And then he'd poured ink on my paper.
And I complained to the teacher.
And the teacher did nothing.
He thought it was funny.
Well, my, my, my mother-- I, I told the, the, the governess, Anna.
And she says, well, there's not much we can do at this point.
We have to just keep quiet.
She felt that it was better just to take it.
And I thought, gee, I can't go through life like this.
This is-- this doesn't seem right.
I remember thinking about that, that it wasn't-- that it was not good.
It was not a good situation.
Once Crystal Night-- November, 1938, Crystal Night took place.
And then they-- all Jews had to leave the schools.
And then the synagogue had set up-- set up a school itself.
No, I guess it was before that.
Because Crystal Night-- at Crystal Night, they burned the synagogue down.
And that's when all schools for Jews ended.
So I must've been-- I missed have started that in September of, of '38.
Because, as I say, I got kicked out of the regular public school.
I just was there a year.
Do you remember Kristallnacht?
Yeah, oh, vividly, very vividly.
All of a sudden, there was a, a knock or a ring on the doorbell.
I went to answer it.
And there was a very large Gestapo man there, SS man.
And he asked for my father.
Well, my father had-- was already deceased.
And I told him my father was dead.
He had been dead a little over a year.
And he proceeded to kick me and punch me.
And I, I went flying up against the wall.
And he was about to stomp on me with his boot.
So my mother came out-- and heard the noise, came out, and said, what's going on?
And the Gestapo man said, I'm looking for Max Freund.
And she told him that he was already dead and to, to leave me alone.
And then he left.
She produced the death certificate to prove that he was really dead.
Why were they looking for him?
To put all Jews in concentration camps.
They put thousands of them in Dachau, which was outside of Munich.
My uncles all were locked up.
Anyone over the age of, I believe, 16 or 18 went into the concentration camp.
And my mother called her girlfriend, who had an automobile.
And she took us out to my father's platoon sergeant from World War I.
And he hid us out at his farm for a week.
That-- it must have been tremendously dangerous for him to hide us out.
And my mother, grandmother, and sister stayed in the farmhouse.
But I went out in a farm field with the farmer.
And I remember he gave me a big hat.
And I could wear the big hat.
It was a big, floppy hat.
And we collected-- he had two bulls pulling the wagon.
And I sat up with him.
And we'd stop it once in a while.
And we'd throw these big sugar beets in the back of the wagon.
And then he proceeded to tell me about World War I and my father, and what they did, and that type of thing.
My father was in the army from 1916 to 1919.
Well, it was his platoon leader.
They were in direct contact all day long, every day, the whole time.
And he told me like my father.
My father, he said, was a-- was a good soldier.
He did his best for his company, or his platoon.
[SIGHS] My father tried to sneak money out to Holland.
And the Gestapo found out about it and called my mother to Gestapo headquarters.
And somehow, they, they got in touch with my father at the hotel.
Right.
And he was just he was sneaking money out.
It was-- it was a capital offense to take money out of the country without the, the authorization.
And don't forget, we were no longer citizens.
So they picked up my mother and held her in detention-- In Munich?
In Munich, at the Gestapo headquarters.
Contacted my father, told him that they knew that he had violated the laws of-- the currency laws by sneaking money out.
And they had my mother in, in the-- in the headquarters.
And he then committed suicide.
Because it was a matter of either that he-- well, he felt that was only a way out.
He wanted to protect my mother.
And he told them that-- evidently, that he was going to take care of the situation.
And that's how he took care of it.
My mother was released.
And he was brought back and then was buried in Munich
In Amsterdam, in a hotel.
I believe he slit his wrists.
Because I, I think by this time, he came to the realization that it was going to be necessary to leave at some time in the future.
And he wanted to make sure that there was sufficient funds to take care of his family.
No.
My cousin, Hans [?
Eichenholdt, ?] was-- knew-- but knew the situation, you know.
What he did, he took it out of the company business.
Because he couldn't take it out of his own personal account because that was blocked.
But he took it from the company, I guess, with the idea that he was going to pay them back.
And somehow, they did an audit on the books.
The government-- the Nazi government came in and did it on the books and found out that there was money missing.
And I guess they put two and two together that here, my father was gone-- had, had traveled and took the money with him.
Because this was-- I assume this was not the first time he had done that.
Well, it was very difficult.
First of all, losing father and husband was terribly tragic for my mother.
My mother, at that time, was 31 years old, two children.
There was no more money coming in because he no longer was employed.
I, I assume he had some insurance.
And they-- my mother lived off of the savings.
And then, when Crystal Night came, then she decided that she was going to leave.
But I-- it was very difficult.
They had-- there was no money coming in.
At Crystal Night, yes.
Yes, we came back to Munich to our apartment.
And that's when my mother decided that it was time for us to, to leave Germany.
Well, she had heard that there was a ship that was leaving and that that ship was going to Havana, Cuba, and that you could purchase entry visas for Cuba.
She had already applied for coming to this country.
Her twin sister was already here with her husband, who was a doctor.
And she made plans.
She-- by, by that time, she already had sent a lot of things to her sister in New Jersey.
A big box-- I remember a huge box of furniture and belongings when she-- at that time, she could still send it out.
So she sent that out because they had-- they came to this country.
And he had just-- he was a doctor and didn't have too much.
So she sent that to him so that they could have some furnishings.
And then in-- by 1938, she had heard about this ship.
She had heard about the visas to Cuba.
And we were going to wait in Havana until our immigration quota number came up.
And then we were going to leave to come to the United States.
Well, I really-- I, I remember them doing a lot of packing.
There was a lot of packing for this huge box that was-- that was leaving.
And then we were left with very little-- and then, as we were about to leave, we packed up two suitcases.
That's all that we could take at that time.
And we had 10 marks, which was about $4.20.
We could leave with that.
So we left a lot of things behind, including our maid and governess.
Yes, I remember we left in the evening and went to the train station.
And we boarded this, this train to go to Hamburg.
And we got there the next day early in the morning.
And my mother got us into this hotel.
Now, you got to realize that most, most hotels would not take Jews.
But my mother knew of a commercial hotel, like a businessman's hotel, where she could go because they knew my father there.
And they allowed us to stay there one night until we could get on the so that we could-- we could leave them.
No, didn't feel safe on the train because we were still in Germany.
There was no rowdyism.
But there was a, a tremendous amount of apprehension because we didn't know if we were getting-- if we were going to get on this ship to leave.
So we were quite concerned about that.
And my mother was very much concerned, my grandmother, also.
I mean, both of them were very apprehensive and very-- it was a very stressful time for both-- for, for both those two ladies, as well as for my sister and myself.
And we felt very much estranged from the whole world, except for our, our closest immediate relatives.
Do you remember the name of the hotel, by the way?
No, I don't.
I know it was somewhere on the docks, close to the docks.
And it wasn't a very nice hotel.
Because I remember my mother told me not to take off my clothes.
We had to-- we had to sleep with our clothes on.
[LAUGHS] How's it going?
